title: Page 1
text:
SiFive FU540 
A RISC-V Based Open-Source Application Processor 
Complete Presentation Study Guide — All 18 Slides 
 
How to use this document 
Each slide section shows exactly what appears on the slide (in blue boxes), followed by what you 
should say for each point (in grey boxes). Q&A questions are shown in blue bordered boxes at the end 
of each slide.

title: Page 2
text:
SLIDE 
1 
Title Slide — SiFive FU540 
 
What's on the slide: 
Main Title 
SiFive FU540 
 
Subtitle 
A RISC-V Based Open-Source Application Processor 
 
Tagline 
Architecture  |  ISA  |  Real-World Impact 
 
Bottom 
[Your Name]  |  Electronics Engg.  |  Feb 2026 
 
How to explain each element: 
🔊 When 'SiFive FU540' appears: 
Open with a hook — don't just say 'my topic is FU540'. Say: 'Every processor you've ever studied — 
the 8085, the 8051, even ARM — is either proprietary or licensed. Companies pay billions to use 
those architectures. What if I told you there's a processor that's completely free, open to anyone, and 
runs full Linux? That's the SiFive FU540 — powered by RISC-V.' 
 
🔊 When 'RISC-V Based Open-Source Application Processor' appears: 
Clarify the two parts: 'RISC-V is the ISA — the instruction set architecture — think of it as the 
language the processor speaks. SiFive FU540 is the actual physical chip that speaks that language. 
It is an application processor — meaning it runs full operating systems like Linux, unlike simple 
microcontrollers.' This distinction sets up everything that follows. 
 
🔊 When 'Architecture | ISA | Real-World Impact' appears: 
Tell the audience the three-part journey: 'Today we'll go from the architectural philosophy of RISC-V, 
to the instruction set design, to a real chip running Linux in the real world. By the end, you'll 
understand every layer — from bits in a register to satellites in orbit.'

Adoption & Future Scope 
 
How to explain: 
🔊 When presenting the Table of Contents: 
Don't just read the list. Say: 'We start with why RISC-V was created — the problem it solves. Then 
we go deep into the architecture — the ISA, registers, pipeline, memory, and privilege levels. Then 
we meet the actual chip — the SiFive FU540 — and compare it against ARM. We finish with real-
world adoption including India's own SHAKTI processor at IIT Madras, and where RISC-V is headed 
next.'

title: Page 4
text:
SLIDE 
3 
Introduction to RISC-V 
 
What's on the slide: 
Heading 
Introduction to RISC-V 
 
Point 1 
Open-source ISA — free for anyone to use 
 
Point 2 
Created at UC Berkeley in 2010 by Krste Asanović & David Patterson 
 
Point 3 
Not a single chip — a specification implemented by real chips like the SiFive 
FU540 
 
Point 4 
Backed by RISC-V International — members include Google, NVIDIA, Intel & 
ISRO 
 
How to explain each point: 
🔊 Point 1 — 'Open-source ISA — free for anyone to use': 
An ISA — Instruction Set Architecture — is the interface between hardware and software. It defines 
what instructions a processor can execute, how registers are organised, how memory is addressed. 
Think of it as the language the processor speaks. RISC-V is an open-source ISA — meaning the 
specification is publicly available, free to use, free to implement, and free to manufacture chips with. 
No licensing fees. No royalties. Anyone from a student in a lab to a national space agency can use it. 
 
🔊 Point 2 — 'Created at UC Berkeley in 2010': 
UC Berkeley already had four generations of RISC research — RISC-I through RISC-IV. RISC-V is 
the fifth generation — hence the Roman numeral V. It was created by Krste Asanović and David 
Patterson — the same David Patterson who co-invented the original RISC architecture in the 1980s 
that ARM is also based on. The motivation was clear: ARM licensing was becoming too expensive for 
universities and startups, and academia needed a clean-slate, unencumbered ISA for teaching and 
research. 
 
🔊 Point 3 — 'Not a single chip — a specification': 
This is the most important conceptual clarification. RISC-V is a blueprint — like a building code. The 
SiFive FU540 is the building constructed using that blueprint. The ISA tells you what instructions 
exist and how they're encoded. The chip decides how to execute them efficiently. This is identical to 
saying 'the 8085 implements the Intel CISC ISA' — you study both the chip and the architecture 
behind it.

title: Page 5
text:
🔊 Point 4 — 'RISC-V International — Google, NVIDIA, Intel, ISRO': 
RISC-V International is a non-profit foundation headquartered in Switzerland — intentionally chosen 
for geopolitical neutrality. It has over 3,000 member organisations across 70+ countries. Being in 
Switzerland means no single country or company controls it. Google uses it in Titan M2 security 
chips. NVIDIA replaced their GPU firmware controllers with RISC-V. ISRO adopted it through IIT 
Madras's SHAKTI project. This breadth of adoption proves RISC-V is no longer a research project — 
it's industrial reality. 
 
Q: If RISC-V is just a specification, how is it different from a processor? 
A: A specification is like a blueprint. RISC-V defines the rules — the FU540 is the building built using 
those rules. The ISA tells you what instructions exist. The chip decides how to execute them 
efficiently. Just like studying the 8085 means studying both the chip and the Intel architecture behind 
it. 
 
Q: What does the V in RISC-V stand for? 
A: It is the Roman numeral 5 — RISC-V was UC Berkeley's fifth generation of RISC processor 
research, following RISC-I, RISC-II, SOAR, and SPUR. It is not an abbreviation.

title: Page 6
text:
SLIDE 
4 
Why RISC-V? — The Problem It Solves 
 
What's on the slide: 
Heading 
Why RISC-V? 
 
Sub-heading 
The problem with proprietary processor architectures 
 
Problem 01 
ARM & x86 — Expensive Licensing 
 
Body 01 
Every company designing a chip must pay royalties to ARM or Intel. This restricts 
innovation, increases cost, and gives full control to a single vendor. 
 
Problem 02 
Proprietary = No Transparency 
 
Body 02 
Closed ISAs mean chip designers cannot modify, verify, or fully trust the 
architecture. Critical sectors like defence, space, and government cannot afford 
this dependency. 
 
Problem 03 
No Customisation Allowed 
 
Body 03 
Proprietary ISAs cannot be extended or modified. Industries like AI, signal 
processing, and space computing need custom instructions — impossible with 
ARM or x86. 
 
Problem 04 
Geopolitical Risk 
 
Body 04 
Nations dependent on foreign-owned ISAs face export control threats. The US 
can legally restrict ARM or x86 licenses to other countries — a critical national 
security vulnerability. 
 
How to explain each point: 
🔊 Problem 01 — 'ARM & x86 — Expensive Licensing':

title: Page 7
text:
ARM Holdings charges companies in two ways — an upfront architecture license fee which can run 
into tens of millions of dollars, and a per-chip royalty of 1–2% of the chip's selling price. For a 
company shipping 100 million chips a year at $5 each, that's $5–10 million annually just in royalties. 
Smaller companies and startups simply cannot afford this. x86 is even more restrictive — Intel 
doesn't license it at all except to AMD under a decades-old cross-licensing agreement. Nobody else 
can legally build an x86 processor. 
 
🔊 Problem 02 — 'No Transparency': 
When you use a proprietary ISA, you trust the vendor completely. You cannot verify there are no 
hidden backdoors, undocumented instructions, or security vulnerabilities embedded in the 
architecture. For applications in ISRO satellites, military systems, banking infrastructure, and medical 
devices — this is unacceptable. RISC-V solves this because the entire specification is public. Every 
instruction, every encoding, every privilege rule is openly documented and verifiable. 
 
🔊 Problem 03 — 'No Customisation': 
An AI chip needs custom matrix multiply instructions. A cryptography chip needs custom AES 
acceleration. A signal processor needs custom FFT instructions. With ARM or x86, adding custom 
instructions requires paying for a special architectural license — extremely expensive and rarely 
granted. RISC-V explicitly reserves opcode spaces for custom extensions. This is why NVIDIA, 
Western Digital, and Google have all added their own custom RISC-V extensions. 
 
🔊 Problem 04 — 'Geopolitical Risk': 
This is the India-specific point that will resonate most with teachers. The US ITAR (International 
Traffic in Arms Regulations) and Export Administration Regulations can restrict the sale of advanced 
processor technology to other nations. India, China, Russia — all face the risk that the US could 
restrict their access to ARM or Intel chips overnight for political reasons. This is exactly why India 
developed SHAKTI on RISC-V — complete independence from foreign ISA vendors. 
 
Q: Can't companies just use open-source ARM cores? 
A: ARM Cortex-M cores have a free tier for some use cases, but the ISA itself is still proprietary. You 
cannot modify the ISA or create your own extensions without ARM's permission and an expensive 
license. RISC-V is different — the ISA itself is open and you can extend it freely. 
 
Q: Is RISC-V completely free even for commercial use? 
A: Yes. The base ISA specification is licensed under Creative Commons. Anyone can implement it, 
manufacture chips, and sell them commercially without paying any royalties to anyone.

title: Page 8
text:
SLIDE 
5 
RISC-V ISA — Base & Extensions 
 
What's on the slide: 
Heading 
RISC-V ISA 
 
Box 01 Title 
Base Integer ISA 
 
Box 01 Body 
RV32I (32-bit) or RV64I (64-bit). Contains just 47 core instructions. Deliberately 
kept small and clean. 
 
Box 02 Title 
Modular Extensions 
 
Box 02 Body 
M = Multiply/Divide, A = Atomic, F = Float, D = Double, C = Compressed, V = 
Vector 
 
Bottom Text 
FU540 implements RV64GC — 64-bit + G (IMAFD) + C (Compressed). Minimum 
required to run Linux. 
 
How to explain each point: 
🔊 Box 01 — 'Base Integer ISA — RV32I / RV64I': 
RISC-V defines a minimal base ISA — RV32I for 32-bit systems and RV64I for 64-bit. RV64I has 32 
registers each 64 bits wide, addressable up to 16 exabytes. The base has only 47 instructions — 
deliberately minimal. RISC philosophy: fewer, simpler instructions execute faster because each takes 
exactly one clock cycle. Compare this to x86 which has over 1,500 instructions — many complex, 
multi-cycle, requiring complex decoding logic. Fewer instructions also means simpler hardware, lower 
power, and smaller die area. 
 
🔊 Box 02 — 'Modular Extensions': 
Instead of one bloated ISA for all purposes, RISC-V lets you pick exactly what you need. An IoT 
sensor only needs RV32I. A GPU needs the V (Vector) extension. A cryptography chip adds custom 
AES extensions. The letter G is a shorthand for IMAFD combined — Integer + Multiply + Atomic + 
Float + Double — the full general-purpose combination. This modular approach is unique to RISC-V 
and is one of its biggest advantages. 
 
🔊 Bottom Text — 'FU540 implements RV64GC':

title: Page 9
text:
Breaking this down letter by letter: RV = RISC-V. 64 = 64-bit registers and address space. G = 
IMAFD shorthand (Integer + Multiply + Atomic + Float + Double precision), plus Zicsr and Zifencei 
system extensions. C = Compressed — 16-bit encodings of common instructions that reduce code 
size by 25-30%. Together, RV64GC is exactly the minimum ISA combination required to boot and 
run the Linux kernel on bare metal hardware. 
 
Q: What is the difference between RV32I and RV64I? 
A: The bit width of registers and the address space. RV32I has 32 registers of 32 bits each, 
addressing up to 4GB of memory. RV64I has 32 registers of 64 bits each, addressing up to 16 
exabytes — practically unlimited. 
 
Q: Can someone add their own custom extensions? 
A: Yes — this is one of RISC-V's biggest advantages. The specification reserves specific opcode 
spaces explicitly for custom extensions. Companies like Google (Titan M2), Western Digital (storage), 
and NVIDIA (GPU management) have all added their own custom RISC-V extensions. 
 
Q: Why do compressed instructions exist? 
A: In embedded systems, code memory (Flash) is expensive and limited. The C extension provides 
16-bit versions of the most commonly used instructions, typically reducing code size by 25-30% 
without sacrificing performance significantly. This is also why RV64GC can run Linux — the kernel 
binary fits in reasonable memory.

title: Page 10
text:
SLIDE 
6 
Architecture Overview 
 
What's on the slide: 
Heading 
Architecture Overview 
 
Box 01 Title 
Harvard Architecture 
 
Box 01 Body 
Separate instruction and data memory buses. Instructions and data fetched 
simultaneously — higher throughput than Von Neumann. 
 
Box 02 Title 
Load-Store Design 
 
Box 02 Body 
Only LOAD and STORE instructions access memory. All computation happens on 
registers — keeps the pipeline clean and simple. 
 
Bottom Text 
32 general-purpose registers (x0–x31). x0 hardwired to zero. Program Counter 
(PC) is separate. 
 
Image 
Block diagram: PC → Instruction Memory → Control Unit → Register File → ALU 
→ Data Memory 
 
How to explain each point: 
🔊 Box 01 — 'Harvard Architecture': 
Point to the block diagram and show the two separate memory blocks — Instruction Memory on the 
left, Data Memory on the right. In Von Neumann architecture, these share one bus — creating the 
Von Neumann bottleneck where the CPU alternates between fetching instructions and accessing 
data. In Harvard architecture, two separate buses allow both simultaneously. In the FU540, this 
manifests as separate L1 instruction cache (32KB) and L1 data cache (32KB) per core — physically 
separate, no bus contention. 
 
🔊 Box 02 — 'Load-Store Design': 
Point to the diagram arrows. Notice computation only happens at the ALU — between the Register 
File and Data Memory. In x86 (CISC), you can do: ADD [memory_address], 5 — reads from memory, 
adds, writes back, all in one instruction. In RISC-V you must: LOAD value from memory into register, 
ADD 5 to register, STORE result back to memory — three separate instructions. This sounds slower

title: Page 11
text:
but actually isn't — each instruction is simple, one cycle, and the pipeline flows cleanly without stalls 
waiting for memory during computation. 
 
🔊 Bottom Text — '32 registers, x0 hardwired to zero': 
Explain x0 as a clever design trick. Instead of a separate clear instruction, you write to x0 (which 
does nothing) or read x0 (always returns zero). Many useful pseudo-instructions are built on x0 — for 
example, NOP is ADDI x0, x0, 0. MV rd, rs is ADDI rd, rs, 0. NOT rd, rs is XORI rd, rs, -1. One 
hardwired register eliminates the need for several dedicated opcodes. 
 
Q: Why exactly 32 registers? 
A: 32 is the sweet spot. More registers means fewer memory accesses (good for performance) but 
each instruction needs more bits to encode the register number (bad for instruction size). With 32 
registers, you need exactly 5 bits per register field, fitting neatly into RISC-V's fixed 32-bit instruction 
width. ARM64 also chose 32 registers for the same reason. 
 
Q: What is the difference between Harvard and Von Neumann architecture? 
A: Von Neumann uses a single shared bus for instructions and data — causing a bottleneck where 
the CPU can't fetch instruction and data simultaneously. Harvard uses separate buses allowing 
simultaneous access. Most modern processors including the FU540 use modified Harvard — 
separate L1 caches but unified L2 and main memory.

title: Page 12
text:
SLIDE 
7 
Register File & Instruction Formats 
 
What's on the slide: 
Heading 
Register File 
 
Text Block 1 
32 general-purpose registers x0–x31. Each 64-bit wide in RV64. x0 permanently 
hardwired to zero. All arithmetic and logic operations happen exclusively on 
registers. 
 
Text Block 2 
6 instruction formats — R, I, S, B, U, J. All base instructions fixed 32-bit width. 
Each format defines specific bit fields for opcode, registers, and immediate 
values. 
 
Callout 1 
32 — General-Purpose Registers | x0 hardwired to zero — x1 to x31 freely usable 
 
Callout 2 
6 — Instruction Formats | R, I, S, B, U, J — all fixed at 32-bit width 
 
How to explain each point: 
🔊 Text Block 1 — 'Register File': 
Introduce the ABI naming convention — engineers don't call them x0-x31 in practice. x0 = zero 
(hardwired), x1 = ra (return address), x2 = sp (stack pointer), x3 = gp (global pointer), x10-x17 = a0-
a7 (function arguments and return values), x8-x9 and x18-x27 = s0-s11 (saved registers preserved 
across function calls), x5-x7 and x28-x31 = t0-t6 (temporary registers). This naming is the ABI — 
Application Binary Interface — the convention that makes compiled code from different compilers 
interoperate correctly. 
 
🔊 Text Block 2 — '6 Instruction Formats': 
Explain each format briefly: R-type (Register) — arithmetic between two registers, e.g., ADD x1, x2, 
x3. I-type (Immediate) — arithmetic with a constant, e.g., ADDI x1, x2, 10. Also used for LOAD 
instructions. S-type (Store) — STORE instructions only. B-type (Branch) — conditional branches, 
encodes PC-relative offset. U-type (Upper Immediate) — loads 20-bit immediate into upper bits, used 
by LUI and AUIPC. J-type (Jump) — unconditional jumps, used by JAL. 
 
🔊 Callout 1 — '32 registers, x0 hardwired to zero': 
The key insight about x0: it's a design choice that simplifies the ISA enormously. Because x0 always 
reads as zero, you can implement pseudo-instructions without adding new opcodes. NOP = ADDI x0,

title: Page 13
text:
x0, 0. MV rd, rs = ADDI rd, rs, 0. BEQZ rs, label = BEQ rs, x0, label. This eliminates dedicated 
opcodes for these common operations, keeping the base ISA at just 47 instructions. 
 
🔊 Callout 2 — '6 Instruction Formats': 
The key insight about fixed format: why split the immediate across fields in S-type and B-type? 
Because by keeping rs1 and rs2 in the same bit positions across ALL formats, the hardware decoder 
can read register addresses before it even knows the instruction format. This speeds up the decode 
stage — parallel register read and format decode. This design cleverness is a hallmark of the RISC-V 
ISA. 
 
Q: Why does RISC-V have 32 registers and not 16 or 64? 
A: 32 is the sweet spot. 16 registers would cause too many memory spills, hurting performance. 64 
registers would require 6 bits per register field in instructions, consuming more of the 32-bit instruction 
encoding budget and leaving less room for immediate values and opcodes. 
 
Q: What does PC-relative offset mean in branch instructions? 
A: Instead of jumping to an absolute memory address, the branch adds a signed offset to the current 
PC value. This makes code position-independent — it can be loaded anywhere in memory and still 
work correctly. Essential for operating system relocation and shared libraries.

title: Page 14
text:
SLIDE 
8 
Pipeline Architecture 
 
What's on the slide: 
Row 1 Heading 
IF → ID → EX 
 
Row 1 Text 
IF: PC sends address to instruction memory, instruction fetched, PC+4. ID: 
Instruction decoded, registers read, control signals generated. EX: ALU performs 
operation, branch target computed. 
 
Row 2 Heading 
MEM → WB 
 
Row 2 Text 
MEM: LOAD reads from data memory, STORE writes. Non-memory instructions 
pass through. WB: Result written back to destination register in register file. 
 
Image 
5-stage pipeline diagram showing 5 instructions in parallel — IF/ID/EX/MEM/WB 
— with RISC-V instruction examples 
 
How to explain each point: 
🔊 Row 1 — 'IF → ID → EX' (Point to the pipeline diagram): 
Look at the diagram — by Cycle 3, three stages are active simultaneously. Instruction 1 is in EX 
while Instruction 2 is in ID and Instruction 3 is just entering IF. The pipeline is like an assembly line — 
every stage works in parallel on a different instruction. IF: The PC holds the address of the next 
instruction. It sends this to the L1 instruction cache, fetches the 32-bit instruction, and simultaneously 
increments PC by 4. ID: The opcode field (bits 6:0) is decoded. Because register fields (rs1, rs2) are 
always in fixed bit positions, they're read from the register file simultaneously with decoding — this is 
why fixed register field positions matter. EX: The ALU executes the operation. For branches, the 
target address (PC + offset) is computed here and the condition evaluated. 
 
🔊 Row 2 — 'MEM → WB' (Point to diagram Cycle 4 and 5): 
MEM: Only LOAD and STORE instructions do real work here — LOAD reads from L1 data cache, 
STORE writes. All other instructions (ADD, SUB, AND, etc.) simply pass through MEM unchanged — 
they just wait. This is important because it means the L1 data cache is only used by memory 
instructions, never conflicting with instruction fetch (Harvard architecture). WB: The result — from 
ALU for arithmetic, from data cache for LOAD — is written into register rd. After WB completes, the 
instruction is retired. The register file now reflects the updated state. 
 
🔊 Image — 'The data hazard hidden in the diagram':

title: Page 15
text:
This is the impressive detail that will stand out. Point to SUB x4, x1, x5 — it needs the value of x1. 
But ADD x1, x2, x3 is still computing x1 in EX when SUB reaches ID. Without intervention, SUB 
would read a stale x1 from the register file. The solution is data forwarding — also called bypassing 
— where the EX output of ADD is directly routed back to the EX input of SUB, skipping the register 
file entirely. The FU540's U54 core implements this forwarding logic in hardware. Without forwarding, 
a pipeline stall (bubble) would be inserted — wasting one cycle. 
 
🔊 Pipeline performance — 'Without vs with pipelining': 
Without pipelining: 5 instructions × 5 stages = 25 cycles. With pipelining: same 5 instructions 
complete in 9 cycles (5 to fill the pipeline + 4 more for the remaining instructions). In steady state, 
one instruction completes every single cycle — CPI = 1 ideally. The FU540's U54 is an in-order 
pipeline — instructions execute strictly in program sequence. This is simpler than out-of-order (like 
ARM Cortex-A72) but consumes far less power, making it ideal for the FU540's embedded Linux 
target. 
 
Q: What is CPI and what is the ideal CPI of a pipelined processor? 
A: CPI = Cycles Per Instruction. Ideal pipelined CPI = 1 — one instruction completes every cycle. In 
practice it is higher due to hazards and stalls. Out-of-order processors can achieve CPI below 1 by 
issuing multiple instructions per cycle (superscalar). 
 
Q: What are the three types of pipeline hazards? 
A: Data hazard: instruction needs result of previous instruction not yet written back — solved by data 
forwarding or pipeline stalls. Control hazard: branch destination unknown during fetch — solved by 
branch prediction and flushing. Structural hazard: two instructions need the same hardware resource 
simultaneously — RISC-V's Harvard architecture eliminates the most common one (simultaneous 
instruction fetch and data memory access).

title: Page 16
text:
SLIDE 
9 
Memory Architecture 
 
What's on the slide: 
Heading 
Memory Architecture 
 
Box 01 Title 
Little-Endian Memory Organisation 
 
Box 01 Body 
RISC-V uses little-endian byte ordering — least significant byte at lowest address. 
FU540 supports 64-bit flat physical address space. 
 
Box 02 Title 
Cache Hierarchy — L1 & L2 
 
Box 02 Body 
32KB L1 I-Cache + 32KB L1 D-Cache per U54 core. All 4 cores share 2MB L2 
cache. E51 has 16KB L1 I-Cache only. 
 
Bottom Text 
MMU translates virtual addresses to physical addresses using page tables. 
Essential for Linux and process isolation. FE310 has no MMU — cannot run 
Linux. FU540 has full MMU — runs Linux. 
 
How to explain each point: 
🔊 Box 01 — 'Little-Endian Memory Organisation': 
Use a concrete example. The 32-bit value 0x12345678 stored at address 1000: Little-endian (RISC-
V): Address 1000 = 0x78, Address 1001 = 0x56, Address 1002 = 0x34, Address 1003 = 0x12. The 
least significant byte is stored first, at the lowest address. Big-endian (used in networking): Address 
1000 = 0x12 (most significant byte first). Little-endian is natural for processors because arithmetic 
hardware processes numbers from the least significant end. ARM and x86 are also little-endian by 
default. 
 
🔊 Box 02 — 'Cache Hierarchy L1 & L2': 
Describe the three-level hierarchy. L1 Cache: Each U54 core has private 32KB instruction cache and 
32KB data cache — single cycle access latency, fastest. This is the modified Harvard architecture — 
separate L1 caches but unified below. L2 Cache: All 4 U54 cores and the E51 share a single 2MB L2 
cache — higher latency but much larger. When data isn't in L1, it's looked up in L2 before going to 
main memory. Main Memory: 8GB DDR4 on the HiFive Unleashed board — hundreds of cycles to 
access. Cache hit in L1 ≈ 1 cycle. L1 miss, L2 hit ≈ 10 cycles. L2 miss, main memory ≈ 100+ cycles.

title: Page 17
text:
🔊 Bottom Text — 'MMU and virtual memory': 
The MMU (Memory Management Unit) is a hardware unit that translates virtual addresses (used by 
programs) to physical addresses (actual RAM). It's essential for three reasons: Process isolation — 
each program thinks it has its own private memory space; the MMU enforces boundaries. Virtual 
memory — programs can use more memory than physically available, with the OS paging unused 
data to disk. Linux requirement — the Linux kernel absolutely requires an MMU. The FE310 has no 
MMU — it cannot run Linux. The FU540's U54 cores have a full MMU (Sv39 scheme — 39-bit virtual 
addresses, 512GB virtual space per process) — which is why the FU540 can run Linux. 
 
Q: Why does cache matter so much? 
A: The CPU operates at GHz speeds but main memory access takes hundreds of nanoseconds — 
the CPU would spend most of its time waiting. Cache bridges this speed gap. The FU540 at 1 GHz 
completes one cycle in 1 nanosecond. DDR4 latency is ~60-80 nanoseconds — 60-80 wasted cycles 
per memory access without cache. With an L1 hit, it's 1 cycle. 
 
Q: What is cache coherency in a multi-core system? 
A: In the FU540's 4-core design, each core has its own L1 cache. If Core 1 and Core 2 both cache 
the same memory location and Core 1 modifies it, Core 2's copy becomes stale. The TileLink 
coherency protocol on the FU540 uses MESI states (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid) to ensure 
all cores always see consistent memory values.

title: Page 18
text:
SLIDE 
10 
Privilege Levels 
 
What's on the slide: 
Heading 
Privilege Levels 
 
Text Block 
RISC-V defines 3 hardware privilege levels — Machine (M), Supervisor (S), User 
(U). Each level has different access to hardware. Higher privilege = more control. 
Lower privilege = more isolation. 
 
Image 
Privilege level diagram: User (top) → ecall → Supervisor → ecall → Machine. 
Return: Machine → mret → Supervisor → sret → User 
 
Callout 1 
M-Mode — Machine Mode | Highest privilege, direct hardware access 
 
Callout 2 
S-Mode — Supervisor Mode | Runs the OS kernel 
 
How to explain each point: 
🔊 Text Block — 'Why privilege levels exist': 
In any real computer, you don't want user applications to have direct access to hardware. A buggy or 
malicious program could crash the system, corrupt memory, or steal data. Privilege levels create 
enforced hardware boundaries — the CPU itself prevents lower privilege code from accessing higher 
privilege resources. This is not a software firewall — it is implemented in hardware. RISC-V defines 
exactly three levels, each with its own set of CSRs (Control and Status Registers) and access 
permissions. 
 
🔊 Image — 'Explaining ecall, sret, mret' (Point to diagram): 
The diagram shows three stacked layers — User at top, Supervisor in middle, Machine at bottom. 
The dashed lines are hardware enforced boundaries. ecall (Environment Call): The arrow going 
downward — from User to Supervisor, and Supervisor to Machine. This is how lower privilege code 
requests services from higher privilege. When a user app needs to read a file, it can't do it directly — 
it executes ecall, which traps the CPU into Supervisor Mode where the OS kernel handles it. sret 
(Supervisor Return): After the OS kernel finishes, sret returns execution to User Mode, restoring PC 
from sepc. mret (Machine Return): After M-Mode firmware finishes, mret returns to Supervisor Mode, 
restoring PC from mepc. 
 
🔊 Callout 1 — 'M-Mode — Machine Mode':

title: Page 19
text:
M-Mode is the highest privilege and the ONLY mandatory level — every RISC-V processor must 
implement it. When a chip powers on, it always starts in M-Mode. Unrestricted access to all hardware 
— all memory, all CSRs, all peripherals. On the FU540, the E51 monitor core always runs in M-
Mode, managing system startup. OpenSBI (the firmware) runs in M-Mode. Key CSRs in M-Mode: 
mstatus (global status), mtvec (trap handler address), mepc (saved PC on trap), mcause (trap 
cause), mie/mip (interrupt enable/pending). 
 
🔊 Callout 2 — 'S-Mode — Supervisor Mode': 
S-Mode is where the OS kernel runs. The kernel has elevated privileges — manages virtual memory 
through the MMU via the satp register, handles system calls from user applications, controls most 
hardware. On the FU540, Linux runs in S-Mode on the four U54 cores. U-Mode (mention verbally): 
The lowest privilege level — all user applications run here. Cannot access hardware directly, cannot 
modify page tables, cannot execute privileged instructions. If it tries, the CPU raises an Illegal 
Instruction exception and traps to S-Mode. 
 
Q: Does every RISC-V chip implement all three privilege levels? 
A: No. M-Mode is mandatory. S-Mode and U-Mode are optional. The FE310 microcontroller only 
implements M-Mode and U-Mode — no S-Mode because it has no MMU and doesn't run an OS. The 
FU540 implements all three — M, S, and U — because it runs Linux which requires all three levels. 
 
Q: What is the difference between an exception and an interrupt? 
A: An exception is synchronous — caused by the currently executing instruction (illegal instruction, 
page fault, system call via ecall). An interrupt is asynchronous — caused by an external event (timer, 
I/O device) unrelated to the current instruction. Both trap to a higher privilege level through the same 
mechanism.

title: Page 20
text:
SLIDE 
11 
Interrupts & Exceptions 
 
What's on the slide: 
Heading 
Interrupts & Exceptions 
 
Entry 1 Label 
Interrupt Triggered 
 
Entry 1 Body 
An external event (timer, I/O, software) signals the CPU. The current instruction 
completes, then the CPU suspends normal execution. 
 
Entry 2 Label 
Context Saved 
 
Entry 2 Body 
PC saved into mepc. Privilege and interrupt status saved into mstatus. mcause 
written with the interrupt or exception code. 
 
Entry 3 Label 
Handler Executes 
 
Entry 3 Body 
CPU jumps to address in mtvec — the trap vector. Handler runs at M-Mode or S-
Mode, reads mcause, services the request. 
 
Entry 4 Label 
Execution Resumes 
 
Entry 4 Body 
mret or sret executed. PC restored from mepc. Privilege level restored. CPU 
resumes from exactly where it was interrupted. 
 
How to explain each point: 
🔊 Entry 1 — 'Interrupt Triggered': 
There are three types of interrupts in RISC-V: External Interrupts — from peripherals (UART, GPIO, 
Ethernet). In the FU540, these are routed through the PLIC (Platform Level Interrupt Controller) 
which manages up to 53 interrupt sources. Timer Interrupts — from the CLINT (Core Local 
Interruptor). Each core has its own timer. The OS programs mtimecmp register — when the free-
running mtime counter reaches mtimecmp, a timer interrupt fires. The OS uses this for preemptive 
multitasking — switching processes at regular intervals. Software Interrupts — one core signals 
another core via the CLINT. Used for inter-processor communication in the FU540's 4-core system.

title: Page 21
text:
🔊 Entry 2 — 'Context Saved': 
This happens automatically in hardware — not in software. The three key registers saved instantly: 
mepc — saves the address of the instruction that was interrupted (for interrupts) or the instruction 
that caused the exception (for exceptions). This is where execution resumes after the handler 
finishes. mstatus — saves the current privilege level and interrupt enable state (MIE bit). mcause — 
written with a 64-bit code identifying the exact cause. Bit 63 = 1 means interrupt, Bit 63 = 0 means 
exception. The remaining bits identify which interrupt or exception. 
 
🔊 Entry 3 — 'Handler Executes': 
mtvec operates in two modes. Direct mode (mode=0): all traps jump to the same base address — the 
handler checks mcause to determine the cause. Vectored mode (mode=1): interrupts jump to base + 
4×cause — each interrupt type has a dedicated handler. Faster for interrupt-heavy systems. The 
FU540 uses a PLIC to prioritise external interrupts — each source has a priority level 1-7, and each 
core has a threshold. Only interrupts above the threshold are delivered. After handling, the core 
writes to the PLIC claim/complete register to signal completion. 
 
🔊 Entry 4 — 'Execution Resumes': 
mret restores everything automatically: PC from mepc (returns to interrupted instruction or next 
instruction depending on exception type), privilege level from mstatus.MPP field, interrupt enable 
state from mstatus.MPIE. For Supervisor-mode traps, sret does the same using sepc and sstatus. 
This clean save-and-restore mechanism is what allows the OS to handle interrupts transparently — 
the interrupted user program never even knows an interrupt occurred. 
 
Q: What is the difference between mret and sret? 
A: mret returns from a Machine Mode trap — restores PC from mepc, privilege from mstatus.MPP. 
sret returns from a Supervisor Mode trap — restores PC from sepc, privilege from sstatus.SPP. Each 
privilege level has its own saved state registers and return instruction. 
 
Q: How does the FU540 handle interrupts across multiple cores? 
A: The PLIC delivers each interrupt to one specific core based on priority and threshold settings 
configured by software. Software interrupts via the CLINT allow cores to signal each other directly — 
for example, Core 0 can send an inter-processor interrupt (IPI) to Core 1 to trigger a task migration or 
cache flush.

title: Page 22
text:
SLIDE 
12 
SiFive FU540 — Introduction & SoC Overview 
 
What's on the slide: 
Heading 
SiFive FU540 
 
Centre Text 
World's first Linux-capable RISC-V SoC, manufactured by SiFive in 2017. 
Features 64-bit multi-core RV64GC ISA. Powers the HiFive Unleashed 
development board. 
 
Card 1 Title 
Core Configuration 
 
Card 1 Body 
4× U54 RV64GC Application Cores | 1× E51 RV64IMAC Monitor Core | Clock: up 
to 1 GHz | Process: 28nm TSMC 
 
Card 2 Title 
Memory System 
 
Card 2 Body 
32KB L1 I-Cache + 32KB L1 D-Cache per core | 2MB Shared L2 Cache | DDR4 
Controller — 8GB RAM | 64-bit address space 
 
Card 3 Title 
On-Chip Peripherals 
 
Card 3 Body 
UART, SPI, I2C, GPIO | GbE — Gigabit Ethernet | PLIC + CLINT | PCIe Gen1 ×4 
 
How to explain each point: 
🔊 Centre Text — 'World's first Linux-capable RISC-V SoC': 
Emphasise the historical significance: 'Before the FU540, RISC-V only existed in FPGAs, simulators, 
and academic papers. The FU540 was the moment RISC-V became real — the first time anyone 
could walk into a store, buy a development board, and boot Linux on RISC-V hardware. It was 
announced at Hot Chips 2017 and shipped as the HiFive Unleashed board in 2018. This single chip 
proved that RISC-V could compete with ARM in a real product.' SoC means System on Chip — all 
major components integrated onto a single die. 
 
🔊 Card 1 — 'Core Configuration':

title: Page 23
text:
The FU540 has a heterogeneous multi-core design — two different types of cores on the same chip. 
The 4 U54 application cores implement RV64GC — full ISA with floating point and compressed 
instructions, full MMU, L1 instruction and data cache. These are the worker cores — they run Linux 
and user applications. The E51 monitor core implements RV64IMAC — no floating point, no MMU, 
instruction cache only. This is the supervisor core — always running in M-Mode, managing system 
startup, power management, and inter-core communication. It never powers down even when U54 
cores sleep. 
 
🔊 Card 2 — 'Memory System': 
The three-level hierarchy: L1 (32KB I-cache + 32KB D-cache per U54 core, 1-cycle access), L2 (2MB 
shared across all 5 cores, ~10 cycle access), DDR4 main memory (~100+ cycle access). The HiFive 
Unleashed provides 8GB DDR4. The E51 monitor core has only a 16KB L1 instruction cache and 
uses tightly coupled SRAM instead of a data cache — appropriate for its M-Mode firmware role which 
doesn't need virtual memory. 
 
🔊 Card 3 — 'On-Chip Peripherals': 
UART: serial communication for debugging — the HiFive Unleashed exposes this via USB for a 
terminal connection. SPI: connects to 32MB SPI Flash where OpenSBI bootloader is stored. I2C: 
low-speed peripheral bus for sensors and small devices. GPIO: general purpose digital I/O pins. 
GbE: Gigabit Ethernet MAC — the HiFive Unleashed has a physical RJ45 port, allowing the board to 
connect to networks and function as a Linux server. PLIC: manages 53 external interrupt sources. 
CLINT: provides per-core timers. PCIe: allows expansion cards to be connected. 
 
Q: What is the difference between the FU540 and the FE310? 
A: The FU540 is an application processor — MMU, cache hierarchy, multi-core, DDR memory 
controller, runs Linux. The FE310 is a microcontroller — no MMU, no cache, no Linux capability, 
executes code directly from Flash. They target completely different applications. The FE310 is like 
Arduino; the FU540 is like Raspberry Pi — but both speak RISC-V. 
 
Q: What is OpenSBI? 
A: Open Supervisor Binary Interface. The M-Mode firmware running on the FU540 at boot. It 
initialises hardware (clocks, DDR, PLIC, CLINT), provides the SBI interface for the Linux kernel in S-
Mode, then hands control to U-Boot bootloader which loads Linux from MicroSD into DDR RAM.

title: Page 24
text:
SLIDE 
13 
FU540 Deep Dive — Multi-Core Architecture 
 
What's on the slide: 
Heading 
FU540 Deep Dive 
 
Bottom Text 
4 U54 application cores share unified L2 cache. E51 monitor core manages 
system-level operations. All 5 cores connected via TileLink coherent interconnect. 
 
Card 1 
U54 Core #1 | RV64GC — Linux + User Apps 
 
Card 2 
U54 Core #2 | RV64GC — Linux + User Apps 
 
Card 3 
U54 Core #3 | RV64GC — Linux + User Apps 
 
Card 4 
(distinct) 
E51 Monitor Core | RV64IMAC — M-Mode Firmware Only 
 
How to explain each point: 
🔊 Cards 1-3 — 'U54 Application Cores': 
Each U54 is a 5-stage in-order pipeline implementing RV64GC. Internal blocks: Branch Predictor 
(static — backward branches predicted taken for loops, forward branches not taken), Integer ALU 
(base integer + M extension), FPU — Floating Point Unit (F and D extensions, IEEE 754 single and 
double precision), 32KB L1 I-Cache (4-way set associative, 64-byte cache lines), 32KB L1 D-Cache 
(4-way set associative, write-back policy). The U54 has a full MMU implementing Sv39 virtual 
memory — this is what enables Linux. 
 
🔊 Card 4 (distinct) — 'E51 Monitor Core': 
Make the visual distinction clear — this card looks different because the core IS different. E51 
implements RV64IMAC — no F or D floating point, no MMU. Has only 16KB L1 instruction cache — 
no data cache (uses tightly coupled SRAM instead). Always runs in Machine Mode — never enters 
S-Mode or U-Mode. Never powered down — maintains system state even when U54 cores sleep. 
Manages the first stage of boot by executing from on-chip Mask ROM. 
 
🔊 Bottom Text — 'TileLink coherent interconnect':

title: Page 25
text:
TileLink is SiFive's open-source on-chip bus protocol. It handles cache coherency between the 4 U54 
cores using the MESI protocol (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid states). If Core 1 modifies a 
cache line that Core 2 also has cached, TileLink sends an invalidation to Core 2's L1. It also handles 
memory-mapped I/O — all peripherals appear as memory addresses on the TileLink bus — and 
arbitrates L2 cache access when multiple cores access simultaneously. 
 
🔊 The Boot Sequence — Most Impressive Detail: 
Walk through the exact boot sequence: 1. E51 wakes first — all U54 cores held in reset. 2. E51 
executes from on-chip Mask ROM — hardwired at manufacture. 3. Mask ROM loads OpenSBI from 
SPI Flash into L2 cache. 4. OpenSBI initialises hardware — clocks, DDR controller, PLIC, CLINT. 5. 
OpenSBI releases U54 cores from reset. 6. OpenSBI hands control to U-Boot in S-Mode. 7. U-Boot 
loads Linux kernel from MicroSD into DDR. 8. Linux boots — mounts filesystem, starts user space. 9. 
User gets a shell prompt. Full Linux on RISC-V. 
 
Q: What does in-order mean and why does the FU540 use it? 
A: In-order means instructions execute strictly in program sequence — no dynamic reordering. Out-
of-order reorders instructions to avoid stalls and keep execution units busy — much higher 
performance but requires a reorder buffer, reservation stations, register renaming — enormously 
complex hardware. The FU540 targets embedded Linux where power efficiency matters more than 
peak performance — in-order is the right trade-off. 
 
Q: What is a cache line and why is it 64 bytes? 
A: The minimum unit transferred between cache and memory. Even if you only need 4 bytes, the 
entire 64-byte cache line is loaded. This works because of spatial locality — programs tend to access 
nearby memory locations together (arrays, structs, sequential code). 64 bytes is the industry standard 
— matching DDR burst transfer sizes for efficiency.

title: Page 26
text:
SLIDE 
14 
FU540 vs ARM Cortex-A72 
 
What's on the slide: 
Row 1 Heading 
SiFive FU540 
 
Row 1 Text 
Open-source RV64GC ISA — no licensing fees | 4+1 heterogeneous cores, in-
order pipeline | 28nm TSMC, up to 1 GHz | Linux capable — Ubuntu, Fedora, 
Debian | TileLink open coherency interconnect 
 
Row 2 Heading 
ARM Cortex-A72 
 
Row 2 Text 
Proprietary ARMv8-A ISA — licensing fees apply | Out-of-order superscalar 
pipeline — higher peak performance | 16nm FinFET, up to 1.8 GHz | Raspberry 
Pi 4, Apple A9, Amazon Graviton | AMBA AXI proprietary interconnect 
 
How to explain each point: 
🔊 Row 1 — 'SiFive FU540 — Open ISA, No Fees': 
Emphasise that zero licensing cost is not just a minor advantage — it is transformational. ARM 
charges $1-10M upfront license + 1-2% per chip royalty. A company shipping 10 million chips at $5 
each pays $500K-$1M annually just to use the ISA. For India's SHAKTI program producing chips for 
ISRO — eliminating this cost while gaining full design transparency is not just financial — it's 
strategic independence. The TileLink interconnect being open-source means even the on-chip bus 
protocol is auditable. 
 
🔊 Row 2 — 'ARM Cortex-A72 — Performance Leader': 
Be analytically honest — teachers appreciate this. The A72 uses a 3-wide out-of-order superscalar 
pipeline — it can issue 3 instructions simultaneously and dynamically reorder them. This gives it 
roughly 3-4× higher single-thread performance than the FU540's in-order U54. The 16nm FinFET 
process (vs FU540's 28nm) gives better power efficiency. 1.8 GHz vs 1 GHz — combined with out-
of-order execution, this is a significant performance gap for compute-intensive tasks. 
 
🔊 The Strategic Conclusion — What to Emphasise: 
This comparison is not ARM vs RISC-V as a winner-takes-all battle. It's about the right tool for the 
right job. For a smartphone needing maximum performance per watt — A72 wins today. For an IoT 
gateway, satellite controller, defence system, or national chip program needing zero licensing cost, 
full auditability, and custom extensibility — FU540/RISC-V wins. The SiFive P670 (2022) already 
competes with ARM Cortex-A75 in performance, showing RISC-V is closing the gap rapidly.

title: Page 27
text:
Q: If ARM is faster and more efficient, why would anyone choose RISC-V? 
A: For many applications raw performance isn't the bottleneck. An IoT gateway running at 500 MHz is 
functionally perfect. The $0 licensing, full openness, custom extension support, and geopolitical 
neutrality make RISC-V the better strategic choice for embedded, space, defence, and national 
semiconductor programs. 
 
Q: What is superscalar execution? 
A: A superscalar processor has multiple execution units and can issue more than one instruction per 
clock cycle. The A72 is 3-wide superscalar — up to 3 instructions per cycle. The FU540's U54 is 
scalar — one instruction per cycle maximum. Combined with out-of-order reordering, the A72's 
effective IPC is significantly higher than the FU540.

title: Page 28
text:
SLIDE 
15 
Real-World Adoption 
 
What's on the slide: 
Heading 
Real-World Adoption 
 
Text Block 1 
RISC-V has moved far beyond academia. Tech giants, government agencies, and 
national semiconductor programs worldwide have committed to RISC-V — the 
fastest growing processor architecture in history. 
 
Text Block 2 
India's SHAKTI processor — developed by IIT Madras, adopted by ISRO — is 
built on RISC-V. India's first step toward processor sovereignty. 
 
Callout 1 
3000+ | Member Organizations | RISC-V International — across 70+ countries 
 
Callout 2 
10B+ | RISC-V Cores Shipped | Projected by 2025 — across all implementations 
 
How to explain each point: 
🔊 Text Block 1 — 'Beyond academia, fastest growing': 
Cover the major adopters with specific reasons for each. NVIDIA: Replaced their proprietary Falcon 
microcontroller cores — used for GPU power management, security, display control — with RISC-V. 
Chosen not for cost (NVIDIA can afford ARM) but for customisability — they added custom 
extensions optimised for GPU workloads. Western Digital: Announced 2019 they would ship one 
billion RISC-V cores per year from storage controllers in hard drives and SSDs. Zero per-chip royalty 
on a billion chips is enormous savings. Espressif ESP32-C3: The most relatable example for 
engineers — the C3 variant of the popular ESP32 IoT chip runs on a single-core RISC-V processor, 
bringing RISC-V to millions of embedded engineers. 
 
🔊 Text Block 2 — 'India angle — SHAKTI at IIT Madras': 
SHAKTI means 'power/strength' in Sanskrit — appropriately named. Developed at IIT Madras under 
Prof. V. Kamakoti (who later became IIT Madras Director in 2021). Funded by MeitY — India's 
Ministry of Electronics and IT. Multiple variants: E-class (IoT), C-class (embedded Linux), M-class 
(application processor). Successfully taped out at SCL Chandigarh — India's government 
semiconductor fab — on 180nm process. ISRO adopted SHAKTI for satellite systems because: full 
design transparency allows radiation hardening modifications, no foreign export restriction risk, and 
long-term supply independence. 
 
🔊 Callout 1 — '3000+ Member Organizations':

title: Page 29
text:
Members span every major tech company — Google, Intel, NVIDIA, Qualcomm, Samsung, IBM, 
Alibaba — and national programs across 70+ countries. RISC-V International's Swiss headquarters 
ensures geopolitical neutrality — even if the US imposed export restrictions, the specification is 
already published and cannot be recalled. This is fundamentally different from ARM (UK/Japan 
company) or x86 (US company) both subject to their home country's export laws. 
 
🔊 Callout 2 — '10 Billion+ Cores Shipped': 
This number includes all RISC-V implementations — from the tiny embedded microcontrollers in IoT 
devices to application processors like the FU540. The fastest any new processor architecture has 
reached this milestone. For comparison, ARM took about 20 years to reach 10 billion cores from its 
founding. RISC-V reached it in roughly 10 years from its 2010 creation, driven primarily by embedded 
implementations in consumer electronics and industrial controllers. 
 
Q: What is ISRO using RISC-V for specifically? 
A: Space-grade processors need radiation hardening and full design transparency — no proprietary 
black boxes in a satellite. RISC-V's open ISA allows ISRO and IIT Madras to verify every aspect of 
the processor design, add radiation-hardening modifications at the RTL level, and manufacture it at 
SCL Chandigarh without foreign export restrictions. SHAKTI's E-class has been used in ISRO 
systems. 
 
Q: Who controls RISC-V — can any one country block it? 
A: No. RISC-V International is headquartered in Switzerland for geopolitical neutrality. The 
specification is already publicly published and cannot be revoked. Even if one country imposed export 
restrictions, the spec is already freely available globally. This is fundamentally unlike ARM (subject to 
UK/Japan law) or x86 (subject to US law).

title: Page 30
text:
SLIDE 
16 
SHAKTI Processor — India's RISC-V 
 
What's on the slide: 
Heading 
SHAKTI Processor 
 
Image 
SHAKTI logo + IIT Madras logo + ISRO logo 
 
Text Block 1 
India's first indigenous RISC-V processor family. Designed at IIT Madras by Prof. 
V. Kamakoti. Taped out at SCL Chandigarh on 180nm process. Variants: E-class 
(IoT), C-class (Linux-capable), M-class (application processor). 
 
Text Block 2 
ISRO adopted SHAKTI for satellite systems — full design transparency, radiation 
tolerance, zero foreign dependency. Part of India's ₹76,000 crore national 
semiconductor mission under MeitY. 
 
How to explain each point: 
🔊 Text Block 1 — 'SHAKTI at IIT Madras': 
Open with the name: 'SHAKTI means power in Sanskrit — and that's exactly what it represents for 
India's semiconductor aspirations.' Kamakoti launched SHAKTI in 2014 — a decade-long project that 
has produced real, physical silicon. The RTL (Register Transfer Level) design files are open-source 
on GitLab — anyone can inspect, verify, and build on SHAKTI. The 180nm process at SCL 
Chandigarh is not cutting-edge by global standards, but it achieves two critical goals: manufactured 
on Indian soil at an Indian fab, and older nodes are inherently more radiation-tolerant — ideal for 
space. 
 
🔊 SHAKTI Variants — Know All of Them: 
E-class: RV32IM, ultra low power, targets IoT and microcontroller applications. C-class: RV64GC 
with MMU — Linux capable, equivalent in scope to the FU540's U54 cores. Most mature and widely 
used. M-class: Out-of-order execution, higher performance — targets application processor market. 
S-class: Multi-core, server-grade. H-class: High performance research grade. The parallel to FE310 
(E-class equivalent) vs FU540 (C-class equivalent) is intentional — the entire RISC-V ecosystem has 
this spectrum. 
 
🔊 Text Block 2 — 'ISRO adoption, ₹76,000 crore': 
Four specific reasons ISRO chose SHAKTI: 1. Radiation hardening — cosmic rays flip bits in space 
(Single Event Upsets). Radiation hardening requires deep design-level modifications — impossible 
with proprietary ISA black boxes. Open RTL allows ISRO to implement triple modular redundancy 
and error correction at the hardware level. 2. Supply independence — ISRO satellites operate 10-15 
years. Proprietary vendors can discontinue chips. 3. Export restrictions — US ITAR could block

title: Page 31
text:
Indian access to advanced processors at any time. 4. Full auditability — no hidden backdoors in 
national security infrastructure. The ₹76,000 crore semiconductor mission and C2S program training 
85,000 engineers on RISC-V creates a self-sustaining ecosystem. 
 
Q: Is SHAKTI good enough to compete with commercial processors? 
A: Current SHAKTI variants are not performance-competitive with ARM Cortex-A or Intel Core. But 
that is not their goal. They target embedded, space, and defence applications where reliability, 
openness, and sovereignty matter more than raw performance. The C-class can run Linux — entirely 
sufficient for satellite control and embedded Linux applications. 
 
Q: What is RTL and why does open RTL matter? 
A: RTL (Register Transfer Level) is the hardware description of the chip written in Verilog or VHDL — 
the source code of the hardware. Having open RTL means anyone can inspect every logic gate, 
verify no backdoors exist, modify for radiation hardening, and synthesise the design at any compatible 
fab. For ISRO satellites controlling national space infrastructure, this level of transparency is non-
negotiable.

title: Page 32
text:
SLIDE 
17 
Future Scope 
 
What's on the slide: 
Heading 
Future Scope 
 
Box 01 Title 
RISC-V in AI & Edge Computing 
 
Box 01 Body 
V-extension enables efficient AI inference. Alibaba XuanTie C910, Ventana Micro, 
Esperanto Technologies — all building RISC-V AI accelerators competing with 
ARM in ML hardware. 
 
Text below Box 
01 
Custom extension space allows chip designers to add dedicated AI instructions — 
impossible with ARM or x86 without expensive custom architecture licenses. 
 
Box 02 Title 
RISC-V in Space & Defence 
 
Box 02 Body 
ESA and ISRO developing radiation-hardened RISC-V processors. Open ISA 
eliminates foreign dependency risks for critical national infrastructure. 
 
Text below Box 
02 
India's C2S program trains 85,000 chip design engineers on RISC-V by 2026 — 
making it the default architecture for India's chip industry. 
 
How to explain each point: 
🔊 Box 01 — 'RISC-V in AI & Edge Computing': 
The V (Vector) extension is RISC-V's answer to AI acceleration. A single vector instruction operates 
on multiple data elements simultaneously — exactly what neural network inference requires (matrix 
multiplications on large arrays). Variable vector length is unique — the hardware decides how wide 
vector registers are, allowing software to run unchanged across different implementations. 
Companies betting everything on RISC-V for AI: Esperanto Technologies (1088 RISC-V cores on 
one AI chip), Tenstorrent (Jim Keller's company — the designer of AMD Zen and Apple A4 
processors — using RISC-V as control core for AI processors), Ventana Micro (high-performance 
RISC-V targeting data centre AI workloads). 
 
🔊 Text below Box 01 — 'Custom AI instructions': 
This connects back to Problem 03 from Slide 4 — the no customisation problem. Custom AI chips 
(NPUs — Neural Processing Units) need custom instructions for matrix multiply, convolution, and

title: Page 33
text:
activation functions. With ARM you need an expensive custom architecture license. With RISC-V you 
add custom instructions in the reserved opcode space — free, fast, fully owned. This is why every 
major AI chip startup is choosing RISC-V as their control processor ISA. 
 
🔊 Box 02 — 'RISC-V in Space & Defence': 
ESA's MICRORISC-V initiative — European Space Agency developing radiation-hardened RISC-V 
for next-generation satellites. Identical motivation to ISRO — European nations cannot depend on 
US-manufactured processors subject to ITAR export control. Two major space agencies 
independently converging on RISC-V is a powerful signal. For defence applications: full ISA 
auditability eliminates backdoor risk. Custom cryptographic extensions can be added without vendor 
permission. Long-term supply independence — no single point of failure. 
 
🔊 Text below Box 02 — 'C2S program, 85,000 engineers': 
The Chips to Startup program creates a self-reinforcing ecosystem: 85,000 RISC-V trained engineers 
→ more Indian RISC-V chip designs → more industry adoption → more demand for RISC-V 
engineers → more training programs. India is industrialising RISC-V expertise at a national scale. By 
2030, India could have the world's largest pool of RISC-V trained engineers — positioning Indian 
semiconductor companies as key players in the global RISC-V ecosystem. This is the vision that 
connects everything in the presentation — from UC Berkeley 2010 to Indian engineers designing the 
next generation of RISC-V chips. 
 
Q: What is the biggest challenge RISC-V faces for mainstream adoption? 
A: Ecosystem maturity. ARM has 30+ years of optimised compilers, debuggers, profilers, OS support, 
and developer familiarity. RISC-V's toolchain (GCC, LLVM, GDB) is solid, but specialised 
performance analysis tools and mature RTOS support are still developing. The gap is closing rapidly 
— Linux kernel support since 4.15 (2018), Android porting started 2023. 
 
Q: What does radiation hardening involve? 
A: Radiation hardening protects against Single Event Upsets (SEUs) — cosmic rays flipping individual 
bits in registers or memory. Techniques include: Triple Modular Redundancy (three copies of critical 
circuits, majority vote decides the output), Error Correcting Codes (ECC) in memory, special transistor 
layouts with larger gate oxides more resistant to particle strikes. All require deep design-level access 
— only possible with open ISA like RISC-V.

title: Page 34
text:
SLIDE 
18 
Conclusion — Thank You 
 
What's on the slide: 
Large Text 
Thank You 
 
Subtitle 
"RISC-V isn't just a processor — it's a movement" 
 
Bottom 
[Your Name] | [Your College] | Electronics Engineering | Feb 2026 
 
Closing Statement — Say This Exactly: 
"From UC Berkeley in 2010 to ISRO satellites today — RISC-V has gone from a research 
project to a global movement in just 15 years. 
The SiFive FU540 was the chip that proved it was real — the world's first Linux-capable 
RISC-V processor. 
And with SHAKTI, India is not just adopting this movement — India is contributing 
to it. Thank you." 
 
🔊 After you finish — How to handle Q&A: 
Stay silent for 2-3 seconds after the closing line. Let it land. Then say: 'I am happy to take any 
questions.' This pause after a strong closing is psychologically effective — it signals confidence and 
gives the audience a moment to absorb what you said. For Q&A, always repeat the question out loud 
before answering — it gives you 3-5 seconds to compose your answer and ensures everyone in the 
room heard the question.
